title: Apply: The Civil War and Emancipation
description: Apply your understanding of the Civil War and Emancipation with this set of multiple-choice questions.
topic_path: America’s Second Founding: Slavery, the Civil War, Emancipation, and Reconstruction
Apply: The Civil War and Emancipation

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an 1861 letter from Lincoln's Secretary of War, Simon Cameron, to Major General Butler.**

GENERAL: The important question of the proper disposition to be made of fugitives from [[☃ definition 1]] in States in insurrection against the Federal Government to which you have again directed my attention in your letter of July 30 has received my most attentive consideration.

[I]in States wholly or partially under insurrectionary control where the laws of the United States are so far opposed and resisted that they cannot be effectually enforced it is obvious that rights dependent on the [[☃ definition 2]] of those laws must temporarily fail; and it is equally obvious that rights dependent on the laws of the States within which military operations are conducted must be necessarily [[☃ definition 3]] to the military [[☃ definition 4]] created by the insurrection if not wholly forfeited by the treasonable conduct of parties claiming them. To this general rule rights to services can form no exception. 

The act of Congress approved August 6, 1861, declares that if persons held to service shall be employed in hostility to the United States the right to their services shall be forfeited and such persons shall be discharged there from. It follows of necessity that no claim can be recognized by the military authorities of the Union to the services of such persons when fugitives.

**According to Cameron, what was the federal government’s stance on slavery in the first year of the Civil War?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: slavery
the act of carrying out some duty
treated as less important than
things that urgently need to be done

choices.content: Enslaved people in Union states were to remain enslaved, while enslaved people in Confederate states could be freed.
All enslaved people could be freed, regardless of their location, as the goal of the Civil War was to end slavery.
No enslaved person could be freed, regardless of their location, as the federal government did not have the power to free them.
Enslaved people in Confederate states could be freed only if they ran away on their own and not through military action.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an 1861 letter from Lincoln's Secretary of War, Simon Cameron, to Major General Butler.**

GENERAL: The important question of the proper disposition to be made of fugitives from [[☃ definition 1]] in States in insurrection against the Federal Government to which you have again directed my attention in your letter of July 30 has received my most attentive consideration.

[I]in States wholly or partially under insurrectionary control where the laws of the United States are so far opposed and resisted that they cannot be effectually enforced it is obvious that rights dependent on the [[☃ definition 2]] of those laws must temporarily fail; and it is equally obvious that rights dependent on the laws of the States within which military operations are conducted must be necessarily [[☃ definition 3]] to the military [[☃ definition 4]] created by the insurrection if not wholly forfeited by the treasonable conduct of parties claiming them. To this general rule rights to services can form no exception. 

The act of Congress approved August 6, 1861, declares that if persons held to service shall be employed in hostility to the United States the right to their services shall be forfeited and such persons shall be discharged there from. It follows of necessity that no claim can be recognized by the military authorities of the Union to the services of such persons when fugitives.

**The policy stance described in the excerpt most directly reflects which other wartime decision Lincoln made?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: slavery
the act of carrying out some duty
treated as less important than
things that urgently need to be done

choices.content: Declaring war against the Confederacy
Suspending the writ of habeas corpus
Issuing the Emancipation Proclamation
Ratifying  the Thirteenth Amendment

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the First Confiscation Act of 1861.**

Be it enacted by the Senate and House of Representatives of the United States of America in Congress assembled, . . . That whenever hereafter, during the present insurrection against the Government of the United States, any person claimed to be held [in] labor or [[☃ definition 1]] under the law of any State, shall be required or permitted by the person to whom such labor or service is claimed to be due, or by the lawful agent of such person, to take up arms against the United States . . . the person to who such labor or service is claimed to be due shall forfeit his claim to such labor, any law of the State or of the United States to the contrary notwithstanding.

**This law is an example of which development?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: slavery

choices.content: The fact that most enslaved people fled to the North during the Civil War
The reluctance to pass any legislation regarding enslaved people
The willingness of the federal government to negotiate with the Confederacy
The use of military powers to free enslaved people in the South

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the First Confiscation Act of 1861.**

Be it enacted by the Senate and House of Representatives of the United States of America in Congress assembled, . . . That whenever hereafter, during the present insurrection against the Government of the United States, any person claimed to be held [in] labor or [[☃ definition 1]] under the law of any State, shall be required or permitted by the person to whom such labor or service is claimed to be due, or by the lawful agent of such person, to take up arms against the United States . . . the person to who such labor or service is claimed to be due shall forfeit his claim to such labor, any law of the State or of the United States to the contrary notwithstanding.

**Which statement best summarizes the intention of this law?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: slavery

choices.content: To increase the president’s wartime powers and effectively end the Civil War with the Confederacy
To give the Union army the ability to liberate enslaved people in the Confederate states
To force border states like Delaware and West Virginia to end the institution of slavery
To give more privileges to the  slave-holding states and encourage them to rejoin the Union

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Abraham Lincoln’s 1862 Presidential Proclamation.**

Now, therefore, be it ordered, first, that during the existing insurrection and as a necessary
measure for [[☃ definition 1]] the same, all Rebels and Insurgents, their aiders and [[☃ definition 2]] within the
United States, and all persons discouraging volunteer enlistments, resisting militia drafts, or
guilty of any disloyal practice, affording aid and comfort to Rebels against the authority of the
United States, shall be subject to [[☃ definition 3]] law and liable to trial and punishment by Courts Martial or Military Commission:

Second. That the [[☃ definition 4]] is suspended in respect to all persons arrested, or who
are now, or hereafter during the rebellion shall be imprisoned in any fort, camp, arsenal, military
prison, or other place of confinement by any military authority or by the sentence of any Court
Martial or Military Commission.

**Which group of people was the main target of this proclamation?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: putting an end to
someone who helps another person do something wrong
military
a legal order that allows an imprisoned person to appear before a court and ask the court to determine if their imprisonment is lawful

choices.content: Enslaved people in the Confederacy
Supporters of the Confederacy
Radical Republicans in the Union
Military personnel for the Union

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from an 1861 letter from Abraham Lincoln to O.H. Browning.**

No doubt the thing was popular in some quarters, and would have been more so if it had been a general declaration of emancipation. The Kentucky Legislature would not budge till that proclamation was modified; and Gen. Anderson telegraphed me that on the news of Gen. Fremont having actually issued [[☃ definition 1]], a whole company of our Volunteers threw down their arms and disbanded. I was so assured, as to think it probable, that the very arms we had furnished Kentucky would be turned against us. I think to lose Kentucky is nearly the same as to lose the whole game. Kentucky gone, we can not hold Missouri, nor, as I think, Maryland. These all against us, and the job on our hands is too large for us.

**Which argument could best use this letter as evidence?**

[[☃ radio 1]]



[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.definition: legal documents that free enslaved individuals

options.explanation: Abraham Lincoln, Letter to O.H. Browning, 1861, [Teaching American History](https://teachingamericanhistory.org/document/to-lose-kentucky-is-to-lose-the-whole-game/).

choices.content: President Lincoln’s ultimate goal was to end slavery in both the border states and the Confederacy.
President Lincoln had no intention of ending slavery, as the Confederacy would never agree to it.
President Lincoln had to suspend some civil liberties in the Union to ensure the United States would win.
The letter does not discuss the suspension of civil liberties.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from an 1861 letter from Abraham Lincoln to O.H. Browning.**

No doubt the thing was popular in some quarters, and would have been more so if it had been a general declaration of emancipation. The Kentucky Legislature would not budge till that proclamation was modified; and Gen. Anderson telegraphed me that on the news of Gen. Fremont having actually issued [[☃ definition 1]], a whole company of our Volunteers threw down their arms and disbanded. I was so assured, as to think it probable, that the very arms we had furnished Kentucky would be turned against us. I think to lose Kentucky is nearly the same as to lose the whole game. Kentucky gone, we can not hold Missouri, nor, as I think, Maryland. These all against us, and the job on our hands is too large for us.

**Which statement best expresses the argument Lincoln makes in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]


[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.definition: legal documents that free enslaved individuals

options.explanation: Abraham Lincoln, Letter to O.H. Browning, 1861, [Teaching American History](https://teachingamericanhistory.org/document/to-lose-kentucky-is-to-lose-the-whole-game/).

choices.content: The goal of the Civil War is to end slavery across the United States.
Slavery is a state issue that the federal government cannot address through national laws.
The Union cannot win the Civil War if the border states join the Confederacy.
People in the border states would support a gradual end to enslavement.

assessment_items.question_content:
[[☃ image 1]]

**Which statement best summarizes the message of the political cartoon?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.explanation: Source: Nast, Thomas. [“Doctor Lincoln's New Elixir of Life—For the Southern States.”](https://gettysburg.contentdm.oclc.org/digital/collection/p4016coll2/id/45/) *New York Illustrated News,* 1862.

options.alt: A political cartoon showing Abraham Lincoln holding a bowl labeled “Emancipation” up to the mouth of a Black man with the word “slavery” printed on his forehead.
options.caption: “Doctor Lincoln's New Elixir of Life—For the Southern States” by Thomas Nast, 1862.

choices.content: The Emancipation Proclamation did not effectively end the institution of slavery in the Confederate states.
During the Civil War, Lincoln aimed to free enslaved people in the Confederate states.
The Emancipation Proclamation increased tensions between the North and the South over the issue of slavery, leading to the Civil War.
Lincoln had no intention of ending slavery across the United States because he was afraid to alienate the border states.

assessment_items.question_content:
[[☃ image 1]]

**Which statement best explains why the artist included “FOR THE SOUTHERN STATES” in the title of the political cartoon?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.explanation: Source: Nast, Thomas. [“Doctor Lincoln's New Elixir of Life—For the Southern States.”](https://gettysburg.contentdm.oclc.org/digital/collection/p4016coll2/id/45/) *New York Illustrated News,* 1862.

options.alt: A political cartoon showing Abraham Lincoln holding a bowl labeled “Emancipation” up to the mouth of a Black man with the word “slavery” printed on his forehead.
options.caption: “Doctor Lincoln's New Elixir of Life—For the Southern States” by Thomas Nast, 1862.

choices.content: Emancipation efforts applied only to states that had seceded from the Union, not to border states.
Emancipation was limited to the South, as slavery existed only in the Confederate states.
Emancipation could be successful only in the Southern states, as these states were forced to follow federal law to rejoin the Union.
Emancipation was meant as a peace offering to the Southern states to convince them to rejoin the Union.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from a letter written by Abraham Lincoln in 1863.**

You ask . . . whether I really claim that I may override all the guaranteed rights of individuals, on the [[☃ definition 1]] of conserving the public safety—when I may choose to say the public safety requires it. This question . . . is either simply a question of *who* shall decide, or an [[☃ definition 2]] that *nobody* shall decide, what the public safety does require, in cases of rebellion or invasion. . . . By necessary [[☃ definition 3]], when rebellion or invasion comes, the decision is to be made, from time to time; and I think the man whom, for the time, the people have, under the constitution, made the commander-in-chief, of their Army and Navy, is the man who holds the power. . . . 

**What event is Lincoln discussing in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: excuse
a statement that something is true
something that is suggested by another statement or event

choices.content: The start of the Civil War
The act of issuing the Emancipation Proclamation
The suspension of habeas corpus
The decision in *Dred Scott v. Stanford*

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from a letter written by Abraham Lincoln in 1863.**

You ask . . . whether I really claim that I may override all the guaranteed rights of individuals, on the [[☃ definition 1]] of conserving the public safety—when I may choose to say the public safety requires it. This question . . . is either simply a question of *who* shall decide, or an [[☃ definition 2]] that *nobody* shall decide, what the public safety does require, in cases of rebellion or invasion. . . . By necessary [[☃ definition 3]], when rebellion or invasion comes, the decision is to be made, from time to time; and I think the man whom, for the time, the people have, under the constitution, made the commander-in-chief, of their Army and Navy, is the man who holds the power. . . . 

**This letter could best be used as evidence to support which argument?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: excuse
a statement that something is true
something that is suggested by another statement or event

choices.content: Lincoln believed that freeing enslaved people would be an advantage for the Union, as it would lead to widespread slave revolts.
The violence of the Civil War caused Northerners to withdraw their support for continued military action.
Congress attempted to reduce Lincoln’s military powers during the Civil War, but Lincoln’s popularity made this impossible.
There were debates about whether Lincoln had the authority to suspend certain civil liberties during the Civil War.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Abraham Lincoln’s 1862 Presidential Proclamation.**

. . .[A]ll Rebels and Insurgents, their aiders and [[☃ definition 3]] within the United States, and all persons discouraging volunteer enlistments, resisting militia [[☃ definition 1]], or guilty of any disloyal practice, affording aid and comfort to Rebels against the authority of the United States, shall be subject to [[☃ definition 2]] and liable to trial and punishment by Courts Martial or Military Commission:

Second. That the Writ of Habeas Corpus is suspended in respect to all persons arrested, or who
are now, or hereafter during the rebellion shall be imprisoned in any fort, camp, arsenal, military
prison, or other place of confinement by any military authority or by the sentence of any Court
Martial or Military Commission.

**Lincoln’s order in the second paragraph relates to which civil liberty?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: required military service
control of an area by soldiers rather than police
people who help others commit crimes

choices.content: The right to a fair process
The freedom of speech
The right to bear arms
The freedom of religion
